---
title: "How These Tools Power Data Science"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import FancyArrowPatch
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # data / generic vectors
HILITE = "#c1121f"   # the highlighted quantity (projection, principal axis, fit)
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # second axis / residual / highlights
GOLD   = "#d4a017"   # third axis / extra series
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"


def draw_vec(ax, tail, head, color=VEC, lw=2.4, label=None, z=5,
             label_offset=(0.0, 0.0), ls="-"):
    """Draw a 2-D arrow from tail to head with a clean head."""
    ax.annotate("", xy=head, xytext=tail,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0, linestyle=ls), zorder=z)
    if label is not None:
        ax.text(head[0] + label_offset[0], head[1] + label_offset[1], label,
                color=color, fontsize=13, ha="center", va="center", zorder=z + 1)
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the **terminal node** of the Geometry-of-Data
> track — the bridge from the toolbox to the rest of the series. It assumes the whole track
> ([GEOM-00] through [GEOM-26]): a dataset is a **cloud of points** in $\mathbb{R}^n$
> ([GEOM-00]); the **dot product** measures length and angle ([GEOM-04]); **orthogonal
> projection** drops a perpendicular onto a subspace, $P = A(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top$
> ([GEOM-06]); a matrix is a **linear map** whose columns say where the axes land
> ([GEOM-08]); the **spectral theorem** factors every symmetric matrix as
> $A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$ with an orthonormal eigenbasis ([GEOM-16]); a **quadratic form**
> $x^\top A x$ is a bowl, saddle, or ridge read off the signs of $A$'s eigenvalues
> ([GEOM-17], [GEOM-18]); the **SVD** $A = U\Sigma V^\top$ works for every matrix and
> reveals its complete geometry ([GEOM-20], [GEOM-21]); the **gradient** points uphill and
> the **Hessian** measures curvature ([GEOM-23], [GEOM-24]); minimizing
> $\tfrac12 x^\top A x - b^\top x$ *is* solving $Ax = b$ ([GEOM-25]); and the **condition
> number** $\kappa = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$ says whether a computer can be trusted
> with the answer ([GEOM-26]). The one question here: *now that the toolbox is built, where
> does each piece reappear across the rest of the series, and how do I choose the right tool
> for a problem I have never seen?* It **sets up nothing further inside this track** — it is
> the explicit hand-off into Regression, Inference, Optimization, Classification,
> Unsupervised Learning, and Causal Inference.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

The Geometry-of-Data track built a toolbox one piece at a time; this article shows the
toolbox at work. Its single claim is that **almost every workhorse method in data science
is one of a small number of geometric operations wearing a statistical costume** — and
once you can see through the costume, you can predict how a method behaves, why it breaks,
and what to reach for instead, on problems you have never met. We make the claim concrete
two ways. First, a **tool-to-consumer map**: projection becomes least squares, the spectral
theorem and the SVD become PCA and covariance analysis, positive-definiteness and Cholesky
become Gaussians and whitening, the gradient and Hessian become optimization and Fisher
information, and the condition number becomes the multicollinearity diagnostic. Second,
**two integrative worked examples** that chain several tools end-to-end on real data —
center, form a covariance matrix, take its spectral decomposition, project onto the top
directions, reconstruct, and read the conditioning — with an honest accounting of exactly
where the geometric assumptions break. We close with an interview-lens roundup of the
standard linear-algebra questions, each answered with the applicability judgment a graduate
of a data-science program is expected to have.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Data science is not a hundred unrelated algorithms; it is a **handful of geometric
operations applied over and over**. Fitting a model is **projecting** a data vector onto a
subspace of explanations. Finding structure is taking a **spectral decomposition** of a
symmetric matrix of pairwise relationships. Decorrelating data, sampling correlated noise,
and certifying a minimum all run on **positive-definiteness** and its **Cholesky** factor.
Training is **descending a gradient** while reading **curvature from the Hessian**.
Knowing whether to trust the answer is reading a **condition number**. Every named method
downstream — OLS, ridge, PCA, the multivariate Gaussian, gradient descent, Newton's
method, Fisher information — is one of these operations in a statistical costume. Learn to
see the geometry, and a method you have never met becomes a problem you have already solved.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The rest of this track derived each operation in
isolation; here we watch them combine, and we make the costume transparent so that when a
later track says "we estimate $\hat\beta$ by least squares" you hear "we project $y$ onto
the column space of $X$," and when it says "we run PCA" you hear "we take the spectral
decomposition of the covariance matrix and keep the top eigenvectors." The single skill
this article installs is **translation** — from statistical vocabulary back to the geometry
you already own.

# Why a bridge article exists at all {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: *why bother collecting the tools into a
map, instead of just meeting each application when it arrives in its own track?* The answer
is the thing that separates someone who can run `sklearn` from someone who can be trusted
with a problem nobody has solved before.

A track built "an inch wide and a mile deep" has a hidden cost. By the time you reach
[GEOM-26] you have proven the spectral theorem, derived the SVD, and computed the gradient
of a quadratic form — each in its own sealed room, each with its own example. The danger is
that the rooms stay sealed: you can prove $A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$ and still not *see* it the
moment someone hands you a covariance matrix. The whole point of geometry-first linear
algebra was that the operations would feel like physical actions on a cloud of points; that
payoff only lands if the actions get *used together*, on data, with stakes.

There is a historical echo here worth keeping in mind. The reason this track exists in its
geometric form at all is that the people who invented these methods were almost never doing
"linear algebra." Gauss and Legendre were reconciling astronomical observations and
inventing least squares — projection — without the word *projection* ([GEOM-06]). Karl
Pearson in 1901 and Harold Hotelling in 1933 were looking for the axes of a cloud of
biometric measurements and invented PCA — the spectral decomposition of a covariance matrix
— decades before anyone packaged it as "eigenvectors of a symmetric matrix." Ronald Fisher
built the information matrix out of the curvature of a likelihood surface — a Hessian —
while thinking about how much a sample could tell you, not about second derivatives of
quadratic forms. **The applications came first; the unifying geometry was extracted
afterward.** This track ran the extraction in reverse so you could learn the geometry
cleanly. This article puts the applications back, so the geometry stops being a museum and
becomes a tool you reach for under pressure.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why should I care about the linear algebra if the library already fits the model for
me?"* Because the library cannot tell you when the model is the wrong tool, and that
judgment is almost always a geometric judgment. *Will this regression's coefficients be
stable?* is "are the columns of $X$ nearly collinear?" — a condition-number question
([GEOM-26]). *How many PCA components should I keep?* is "where do the eigenvalues of the
covariance matrix fall off a cliff?" ([GEOM-16], [GEOM-21]). *Why did my optimizer crawl?*
is "is the Hessian ill-conditioned — a long thin valley?" ([GEOM-24], [GEOM-26]). The
library returns numbers; the geometry tells you whether the numbers mean anything. An
interviewer asking this is checking whether you can diagnose, not just call.
:::

# The map: each tool and the methods it powers {#sec-map}

We start with the bird's-eye view, then spend the rest of the article earning it. @fig-map
is the single picture to carry out of this track: the left column is the geometric tool we
built, the right column is the statistical method it becomes, and the edge is the
translation. Read it as "the method *is* the tool, renamed."

```{python}
#| label: fig-map
#| fig-cap: "The tool-to-consumer map. Each geometric operation on the left (built in this track) reappears on the right as a named data-science method (owned by another track). The edge label is the translation: the method is the operation in a statistical costume. This is the single diagram to remember from the Geometry-of-Data track."
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9.0, 6.4))
ax.axis("off")
ax.set_xlim(0, 10); ax.set_ylim(0, 10)

tools = [
    ("Orthogonal projection\n$P=A(A^\\top A)^{-1}A^\\top$\n[GEOM-06]", 9.2),
    ("Spectral theorem / SVD\n$A=Q\\Lambda Q^\\top,\\;A=U\\Sigma V^\\top$\n[GEOM-16],[GEOM-20]", 7.4),
    ("Positive-definite\n& Cholesky $A=LL^\\top$\n[GEOM-18],[GEOM-19]", 5.6),
    ("Gradient & Hessian\n$\\nabla f,\\;\\nabla^2 f$\n[GEOM-23],[GEOM-24]", 3.8),
    ("Condition number\n$\\kappa=\\sigma_{\\max}/\\sigma_{\\min}$\n[GEOM-26]", 2.0),
]
consumers = [
    ("Least squares / OLS\nleast squares = projection\n[REGR-04],[REGR-05]", 9.2, "best fit in a subspace"),
    ("PCA / covariance\ntop eigenvectors of $\\Sigma$\n[UNSP-07]", 7.4, "axes of the cloud"),
    ("Gaussians, GLS, whitening\n$\\Sigma^{-1/2}$, sampling $Lz$\n[REGR-11],[PROB-17]", 5.6, "decorrelate / simulate"),
    ("Gradient descent, Newton,\nFisher information\n[OPTM],[INFR-B3]", 3.8, "train / curvature = information"),
    ("Multicollinearity,\nnumerical care\n[REGR-07]", 2.0, "is the answer trustworthy?"),
]

for txt, y in tools:
    ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((0.2, y-0.75), 3.5, 1.5, fc=PALE, ec=VEC, lw=1.4, alpha=0.55))
    ax.text(1.95, y, txt, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=8.4)
for txt, y, edge in consumers:
    ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((6.3, y-0.75), 3.5, 1.5, fc="#f6e7c1", ec=GOLD, lw=1.4, alpha=0.7))
    ax.text(8.05, y, txt, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=8.4)
    ax.annotate("", xy=(6.25, y), xytext=(3.75, y),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=HILITE, lw=2.0))
    ax.text(5.0, y+0.32, edge, ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=7.6, color=HILITE, style="italic")

ax.text(1.95, 10.0, "GEOMETRIC TOOL", ha="center", fontsize=10, weight="bold", color=VEC)
ax.text(8.05, 10.0, "DATA-SCIENCE METHOD", ha="center", fontsize=10, weight="bold", color=GOLD)
plt.tight_layout()
```

We take the five edges of @fig-map one at a time. Each subsection is short on purpose — the
*derivation* of each tool already lives in its own article; here we only make the
translation explicit and say precisely where the seam is between what Geometry owns and
what the consuming track owns.

## Projection becomes least squares {#sec-map-proj}

In [GEOM-06] we asked: given a point $y$ and a subspace, what is the closest point in the
subspace, and why is "drop a perpendicular" the answer? We derived that the closest point is
the **orthogonal projection** $\hat y = Py$ with $P = A(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top$, characterized
by the residual $y - \hat y$ being orthogonal to every column of $A$ — the **normal
equations** $A^\top(y - A\hat c) = 0$, i.e. $A^\top A\,\hat c = A^\top y$.

Regression is this operation with the columns renamed. Stack the predictor columns into a
**design matrix** $X$ and the outcomes into a vector $y$; the model $\hat y = X\beta$ says
the fitted values are forced to lie in the **column space** of $X$ — the subspace of all
linear combinations of predictors. Ordinary least squares chooses the $\beta$ that minimizes
$\lVert y - X\beta\rVert^2$, which is the squared distance from $y$ to that subspace.
Minimizing distance to a subspace *is* projecting (@thm-ls-is-proj makes this exact). The
normal equations become $X^\top X\,\hat\beta = X^\top y$, and the fitted vector
$\hat y = X(X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top y = Py$ is literally the projection matrix of [GEOM-06]
applied to $y$. The statistical word "fit" and the geometric word "project" name the same
arrow. The full statistical reading — that this is also Gaussian maximum likelihood, what
its sampling distribution is, when it is the best linear unbiased estimator — is
**Regression's** to own ([REGR-04], [REGR-05]); the geometric skeleton is ours.

::: {#thm-ls-is-proj}
**Theorem (least squares is projection).** Let $X$ be $n \times p$ with linearly
independent columns and $y \in \mathbb{R}^n$. The vector $\hat\beta$ minimizing
$\lVert y - X\beta\rVert^2$ over all $\beta \in \mathbb{R}^p$ is the unique solution of
$X^\top X\,\hat\beta = X^\top y$, and the fitted vector $\hat y = X\hat\beta$ is the
orthogonal projection of $y$ onto the column space $\mathcal{C}(X)$.
:::

::: {.proof}
We show the minimizer of the squared distance is exactly the point whose residual is
orthogonal to the subspace, and that this point is the projection — closing the loop
between the *optimization* statement (minimize $\lVert y - X\beta\rVert^2$) and the
*geometric* statement (project) without assuming either.

Write the objective as a function of $\beta$ and expand the squared norm using
$\lVert v\rVert^2 = v^\top v$:
$$
S(\beta) = \lVert y - X\beta\rVert^2 = (y - X\beta)^\top(y - X\beta)
= y^\top y - 2\beta^\top X^\top y + \beta^\top X^\top X\,\beta .
$$
The middle term used $y^\top X\beta = \beta^\top X^\top y$ (a scalar equals its own
transpose). This is a **quadratic form** in $\beta$ of exactly the shape
$\tfrac12 \beta^\top (2X^\top X)\beta - (2X^\top y)^\top\beta + \text{const}$, so by the
gradient identity for quadratic forms proved component-by-component in [GEOM-25]
($\nabla(\tfrac12\beta^\top M\beta - b^\top\beta) = M\beta - b$ for symmetric $M$, here
with $M = 2X^\top X$ and $b = 2X^\top y$),
$$
\nabla S(\beta) = 2X^\top X\,\beta - 2X^\top y .
$$
Setting the gradient to zero — the first-order optimality condition of [GEOM-23] — gives
the normal equations
$$
X^\top X\,\hat\beta = X^\top y .
$$ {#eq-normal-multi}
Because the columns of $X$ are linearly independent, $X^\top X$ is positive-definite
(proved in [GEOM-18]: $\beta^\top X^\top X\beta = \lVert X\beta\rVert^2 > 0$ for
$\beta \neq 0$ exactly when $X\beta \neq 0$, which independence guarantees). A
positive-definite matrix is invertible, so $\hat\beta = (X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top y$ is the
unique stationary point; and since the Hessian $\nabla^2 S = 2X^\top X$ is positive-definite
everywhere, the second-order test of [GEOM-24] certifies this stationary point is the global
minimum (the surface is a bowl). Finally, the residual at $\hat\beta$ satisfies
$X^\top(y - X\hat\beta) = X^\top y - X^\top X\hat\beta = 0$ by the normal equations — the
residual is orthogonal to every column of $X$, hence to all of $\mathcal{C}(X)$. That
orthogonality is precisely the defining property of the orthogonal projection onto
$\mathcal{C}(X)$ proved in [GEOM-06], so $\hat y = X\hat\beta = Py$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Notice the proof itself chained four articles — projection ([GEOM-06]), the quadratic-form
gradient ([GEOM-25]), positive-definiteness ([GEOM-18]), and the Hessian test ([GEOM-24]) —
to close one statistical claim. That chaining is the entire thesis of this article in
miniature.

## The spectral theorem and SVD become PCA and covariance analysis {#sec-map-pca}

In [GEOM-16] we proved that every real symmetric matrix factors as $A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$ —
an **orthonormal** basis of eigenvectors (the columns of $Q$) and real eigenvalues (the
diagonal of $\Lambda$) — and read it geometrically as "rotate to the eigenaxes, scale along
each, rotate back, no shear ever." In [GEOM-17] we saw that $u^\top A u$ for a unit vector
$u$ is the eigenvalue-weighted combination of $u$'s coordinates in the eigenbasis, maximized
by pointing $u$ along the top eigenvector.

A **covariance matrix** $\Sigma$ (Probability owns its meaning — see [PROB-17]) is symmetric
and positive-semidefinite, and the quadratic form $u^\top \Sigma u$, for a unit vector $u$,
is exactly the variance of the data projected onto direction $u$. That one-line bridge is
worth seeing rather than asserting. Let the centered data rows be
$x_1,\dots,x_n \in \mathbb{R}^p$ (each $x_i$ already has the column means subtracted, so
$\tfrac1n\sum_i x_i = 0$), and let $\Sigma = \tfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_i x_i x_i^\top$. Projecting a
point $x_i$ onto the unit direction $u$ gives the scalar coordinate $u^\top x_i$ (the dot
product of [GEOM-04]); because the data are centered, these coordinates also have mean zero,
$\tfrac1n\sum_i u^\top x_i = u^\top(\tfrac1n\sum_i x_i) = 0$. The sample variance of those
projected coordinates is therefore
$$
\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(u^\top x_i\bigr)^2
= \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n} u^\top x_i\,x_i^\top u
= u^\top\!\left(\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i x_i^\top\right)\! u
= u^\top \Sigma u ,
$$
where the first equality writes the scalar square $(u^\top x_i)^2$ as
$(u^\top x_i)(x_i^\top u)$ — the same scalar, since $u^\top x_i = x_i^\top u$ — and the third
pulls the constant vector $u$ outside the sum. So maximizing the projected variance over unit
directions is exactly maximizing the quadratic form $u^\top\Sigma u$ subject to
$\lVert u\rVert = 1$. [GEOM-17] solved precisely that constrained maximization — diagonalize
with the spectral theorem, $u^\top\Sigma u = \sum_k \lambda_k\,(q_k^\top u)^2$, a weighted
average of the eigenvalues that is largest when all the weight sits on the largest one — so
the maximizer is the **top eigenvector** of $\Sigma$ and the maximal variance is the **top
eigenvalue**. That single
sentence is PCA. Principal component analysis is the spectral decomposition of the
covariance matrix, keeping the eigenvectors with the largest eigenvalues because those are
the directions in which the cloud is most stretched. Equivalently, working from the centered
data matrix $X_c$ directly, the **SVD** $X_c = U\Sigma_{\text{sv}}V^\top$ ([GEOM-20]) hands
the principal directions as the columns of $V$ and the variances as $\sigma^2/(n-1)$ — the
SVD route avoids ever forming $X_c^\top X_c$ and so is the numerically preferred one (a
conditioning argument from [GEOM-26], revisited in @sec-conditioning-example).

The seam, as the track's ownership table fixes it: **Geometry owns "best rank-$k$
approximation of a matrix" and "eigenvectors of a symmetric matrix"** ([GEOM-16],
[GEOM-20], [GEOM-21]); **Unsupervised Learning owns PCA *as a statistical procedure*** —
why we center, what the components *mean*, how to choose $k$ with a scree plot, biplots,
whitening, the probabilistic model ([UNSP-07], [UNSP-08]). UL links back here for the
algebra rather than re-deriving the SVD. We work a full PCA-as-geometry example in
@sec-faithful-pca.

## Positive-definiteness and Cholesky become Gaussians, whitening, and GLS {#sec-map-pd}

In [GEOM-18] we characterized **positive-definite** matrices as those with all-positive
eigenvalues whose quadratic form is a bowl with a unique bottom, and in [GEOM-19] we factored
any such matrix as $A = LL^\top$ (**Cholesky**, $L$ lower-triangular) and built its
**matrix square root** $A^{1/2} = Q\Lambda^{1/2}Q^\top$ from the spectral theorem.

These are the engine of everything Gaussian. A valid multivariate Gaussian *requires* a
positive-definite covariance $\Sigma$ — the density's exponent is the negative quadratic
form $-\tfrac12 (x-\mu)^\top\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)$, and it is a genuine bell-shaped bump
(integrable, with a unique mode) exactly when that form is a bowl, i.e. when $\Sigma$ is
positive-definite. To **sample** a correlated Gaussian you draw a vector $z$ of independent
standard normals — so its covariance is the identity, $\operatorname{Cov}(z) = I$ — and form
$\mu + Lz$. The reason this works is the linear-transform rule for covariance, which is
short enough to derive here rather than borrow. For any fixed matrix $M$ and random vector
$z$ with mean zero, the transformed vector $Mz$ also has mean zero
($\mathbb{E}[Mz] = M\,\mathbb{E}[z] = 0$, since $M$ is constant), and its covariance is the
expected outer product
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(Mz) = \mathbb{E}\!\bigl[(Mz)(Mz)^\top\bigr]
= \mathbb{E}\!\bigl[Mz\,z^\top M^\top\bigr]
= M\,\mathbb{E}\!\bigl[z z^\top\bigr]\,M^\top
= M\operatorname{Cov}(z)\,M^\top ,
$$
using $(Mz)^\top = z^\top M^\top$ and pulling the constant matrices $M, M^\top$ outside the
expectation (linearity). Setting $M = L$ and $\operatorname{Cov}(z) = I$ gives
$\operatorname{Cov}(Lz) = L\,I\,L^\top = LL^\top = \Sigma$ — Cholesky manufactures exactly the
target correlation. (The shift by $\mu$ only moves the mean and leaves the covariance
unchanged, since subtracting the mean is built into the definition of covariance.) To **whiten** (decorrelate) data
you multiply by $\Sigma^{-1/2}$, turning a tilted elliptical cloud into a round one; this is
the move generalized least squares ([REGR-11]) uses to convert correlated-error regression
back into ordinary least squares. Probability and Inference own the *statistics* of the
Gaussian; Regression owns GLS; Geometry owns the factorizations they all call. We use the
square-root/whitening move inside the worked example of @sec-faithful-pca.

## Gradient and Hessian become optimization and Fisher information {#sec-map-grad}

In [GEOM-23] the **gradient** $\nabla f$ pointed in the direction of steepest ascent and was
perpendicular to level sets; in [GEOM-24] the **Hessian** $\nabla^2 f$ measured curvature,
and its definiteness ran the second-derivative test — positive-definite Hessian means a
bowl, hence a local minimum.

Training a model is descending this geometry. **Gradient descent** takes steps along
$-\nabla f$ (downhill); **Newton's method** preconditions the step by the inverse Hessian,
$-(\nabla^2 f)^{-1}\nabla f$, which is exactly the step that jumps straight to the bottom of
the local quadratic model (the quadratic-form minimizer of [GEOM-25]). The *algorithms* —
step sizes, line search, momentum, convergence rates — are **Optimization's** to own; the
*geometry* they walk on is ours. On the statistics side, **Fisher information** is the
Hessian in a costume: it is (the expectation of) the negative curvature of the
log-likelihood surface at its peak, so a sharply curved likelihood (large Hessian) means the
data pin the parameter down tightly (high information, low variance). Inference builds the
statistical object ([INFR-B3]); Geometry supplied the Hessian-as-curvature picture
([GEOM-24], [GEOM-25]) it stands on. The conditioning of the Hessian — the eigenvalue ratio
of [GEOM-26] — is what decides whether gradient descent crawls down a long thin valley or
rolls straight to the bottom, the topic of @sec-conditioning-example.

## The condition number becomes the multicollinearity diagnostic {#sec-map-cond}

In [GEOM-26] the **condition number** $\kappa(A) = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$ measured the
worst-case amplification of relative error when solving $Ax = b$, and its geometry was a
stretched-out ellipse: near-parallel columns make $\sigma_{\min}$ tiny, $\kappa$ huge, and
the solution wildly sensitive to small data wiggles.

In regression this *is* **multicollinearity**. When predictor columns are nearly linearly
dependent, $X^\top X$ has a tiny smallest eigenvalue, $\kappa(X^\top X)$ explodes, and the
coefficient estimates swing violently from sample to sample even though the *fit* is fine.
The cure — ridge regression's $X^\top X + \lambda I$ — works precisely because adding
$\lambda$ to every eigenvalue lifts the smallest one off the floor and slashes the condition
number. Regression owns the statistical story (variance inflation, confidence-interval
blowup, the bias–variance trade of ridge — [REGR-07], [REGR-14]); Geometry owns the
condition number and the "solve, don't invert" judgment. We measure $\kappa$ live in
@sec-conditioning-example.

# Worked example I: the geometry pipeline on a real cloud {#sec-faithful-pca}

We now chain four tools end to end on a genuine dataset, doing every numerical step in the
open: **center $\to$ covariance $\to$ spectral decomposition $\to$ project $\to$
reconstruct**, then whiten, then read off the conditioning. The data are the classic Old
Faithful geyser measurements — for each eruption, the *duration* (minutes) and the *waiting
time* until the next eruption (minutes). These two are strongly related (long waits follow
long eruptions), so the cloud is a tilted ellipse — exactly the regime where the spectral
picture earns its keep, and honest enough that we will see where it strains.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pca-pipeline
#| fig-cap: "The geometry pipeline on the Old Faithful cloud. **Left:** centered data (blue) with the two principal axes drawn as arrows scaled to one standard deviation along each — the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, longest along the direction of maximum variance. **Right:** the rank-1 reconstruction (red) keeping only the top principal component; every point is forced onto the dominant axis (grey line), and the dropped perpendiculars are exactly the projection residuals of [GEOM-06]. The geometry is honest here: the cloud genuinely has one dominant direction, so one component captures most of it — but the right panel also shows the cost, the spread that the single axis throws away."
# Old Faithful: (eruption duration, waiting time). A representative slice.
dur = np.array([3.6,1.8,3.33,2.28,4.53,2.88,4.7,3.6,1.95,4.35,
                1.83,3.92,4.2,1.75,4.27,1.67,4.5,1.73,4.62,3.43,
                4.25,1.68,3.92,3.68,3.1,4.43,2.72,4.27,1.85,3.95], dtype=float)
wait = np.array([79,54,74,62,85,55,88,85,51,85,
                 54,84,78,47,83,52,84,53,84,70,
                 84,52,79,76,64,86,57,82,52,80], dtype=float)
D = np.column_stack([dur, wait])
n = D.shape[0]
mu = D.mean(axis=0)
Dc = D - mu                                  # center
Sigma = (Dc.T @ Dc) / (n - 1)                # sample covariance (2x2)
evals, evecs = np.linalg.eigh(Sigma)         # ascending; eigh -> symmetric
order = np.argsort(evals)[::-1]              # largest first
evals = evals[order]; evecs = evecs[:, order]

# Standardize columns to comparable scale for a clean picture of the *direction*.
scale = Dc.std(axis=0, ddof=1)
Z = Dc / scale
Sz = (Z.T @ Z) / (n - 1)
ev_z, V_z = np.linalg.eigh(Sz)
oz = np.argsort(ev_z)[::-1]; ev_z = ev_z[oz]; V_z = V_z[:, oz]

# Rank-1 reconstruction in the standardized space, then map back.
pc1 = V_z[:, [0]]                            # top principal direction (unit)
coords = Z @ pc1                             # scores on PC1
Z_rec = coords @ pc1.T                       # rank-1 reconstruction
D_rec = Z_rec * scale + mu                   # back to original units
Zc = Z                                       # standardized centered cloud

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 4.1))

axL.scatter(Zc[:, 0], Zc[:, 1], color=VEC, s=28, alpha=0.85, zorder=3, label="centered data")
for k, col, lab in [(0, HILITE, "PC1"), (1, ACCENT, "PC2")]:
    v = V_z[:, k] * np.sqrt(ev_z[k])
    draw_vec(axL, (0, 0), (v[0], v[1]), color=col, lw=3.0,
             label=f"{lab} ($\\sqrt{{\\lambda}}={np.sqrt(ev_z[k]):.2f}$)",
             label_offset=(0.25*np.sign(v[0]+0.1), 0.18*np.sign(v[1]+0.1)))
axL.set_aspect("equal"); axL.set_title("Centered cloud + principal axes")
axL.set_xlabel("standardized duration"); axL.set_ylabel("standardized wait")
axL.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)

axR.scatter(D[:, 0], D[:, 1], color=VEC, s=28, alpha=0.6, zorder=3, label="original")
axR.scatter(D_rec[:, 0], D_rec[:, 1], color=HILITE, s=30, zorder=4, marker="D",
            label="rank-1 reconstruction")
for p, q in zip(D, D_rec):
    axR.plot([p[0], q[0]], [p[1], q[1]], color=GREY, lw=1.0, zorder=2)
# draw the PC1 line through the mean (in original units)
t = np.linspace(-2.4, 2.4, 50)
line = mu + (t[:, None] * (V_z[:, 0] * scale))
axR.plot(line[:, 0], line[:, 1], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.2, zorder=1)
axR.set_title("Project onto PC1, reconstruct")
axR.set_xlabel("duration (min)"); axR.set_ylabel("wait (min)")
axR.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"mean (duration, wait)   = ({mu[0]:.3f}, {mu[1]:.3f})")
print(f"covariance matrix Sigma =\n{Sigma}")
print(f"eigenvalues  (orig units) = {evals}")
print(f"eigenvectors (cols)      =\n{evecs}")
print(f"variance fraction in PC1 (standardized) = {ev_z[0]/ev_z.sum():.4f}")
```

Walk the pipeline the code just executed, tying each step to the article that owns it.

**Step 1 — center.** We subtract the column means $\mu = (\bar{\text{dur}}, \bar{\text{wait}})$
so the cloud is anchored at the origin; centering is what makes the covariance matrix and the
eigen-directions about *spread* rather than *location*. (Why centering is the right
pre-processing for variance analysis is Unsupervised Learning's point to make in [UNSP-07];
geometrically it is just translating the cloud to the origin, [GEOM-00].)

**Step 2 — covariance.** We form $\Sigma = \tfrac{1}{n-1}X_c^\top X_c$, a $2\times 2$
**symmetric positive-semidefinite** matrix ([GEOM-18] guarantees $X_c^\top X_c$ is PSD). Its
diagonal entries are the two variances and its off-diagonal entry is the covariance; here it
is strongly positive, confirming long eruptions and long waits travel together.

**Step 3 — spectral decomposition.** We take the eigendecomposition $\Sigma = Q\Lambda Q^\top$
with `np.linalg.eigh` (the routine for *symmetric* matrices, which returns an orthonormal $Q$
exactly as the spectral theorem of [GEOM-16] promises). The eigenvector with the larger
eigenvalue is the long axis of the ellipse — the direction of maximum variance, drawn as the
red **PC1** arrow in the left panel. The variance fraction printed shows PC1 alone carries
the overwhelming majority of the spread: this cloud is *almost* one-dimensional.

**Step 4 — project and reconstruct.** The rank-1 reconstruction is the **orthogonal
projection** of [GEOM-06] onto the line spanned by PC1: every point is replaced by its closest
point on that line, and the grey segments in the right panel are the projection residuals —
the perpendicular gaps we proved are orthogonal to the subspace. Keeping only PC1 is the
best rank-1 approximation of the centered data (Eckart–Young, [GEOM-21]); the residuals are
the price.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why did you standardize the columns before taking eigenvectors, and when would that be
wrong?"* Duration is measured in single minutes and waiting time in tens of minutes, so on
the raw scale the wait variable's larger numbers would dominate the covariance purely because
of its units — the top eigenvector would chase the variable with the bigger numbers, not the
one with more *relative* structure. Standardizing (dividing each column by its standard
deviation) puts them on equal footing, which is the right default when variables have
incommensurable units. But it is **not** always right: if the variables are already in the
same units and their differing variances are *real signal* (e.g. pixel intensities, returns
of comparable assets), standardizing throws that signal away. The choice between covariance
PCA and correlation PCA is a genuine modeling decision Unsupervised Learning takes up in
[UNSP-08]; geometrically it is just whether you stretch the axes before finding the cloud's
direction.
:::

## Whitening the same cloud {#sec-whiten}

The same spectral decomposition gives the whitening transform for free. Whitening sends the
tilted ellipse to a round, unit-variance, uncorrelated cloud by multiplying centered data by
$\Sigma^{-1/2} = Q\Lambda^{-1/2}Q^\top$ — the inverse matrix square root of [GEOM-19], built
from the very $Q, \Lambda$ we already computed.

```{python}
#| label: fig-whiten
#| fig-cap: "Whitening as the inverse matrix square root in action. **Left:** the centered Old Faithful cloud is a tilted ellipse — the variables are correlated. **Right:** after applying $W=\\Sigma^{-1/2}=Q\\Lambda^{-1/2}Q^\\top$, the cloud is round, with unit variance in every direction and zero correlation. This is the exact move generalized least squares uses to turn correlated-error regression into ordinary least squares, and the move that turns a general Gaussian into a standard one."
# Build Sigma^{-1/2} from the (original-unit) spectral decomposition.
Lam = np.diag(evals)
Q = evecs
Sigma_inv_sqrt = Q @ np.diag(evals ** -0.5) @ Q.T
W = Dc @ Sigma_inv_sqrt.T                      # whitened coordinates
cov_white = (W.T @ W) / (n - 1)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.0))
axL.scatter(Dc[:, 0], Dc[:, 1], color=VEC, s=28, alpha=0.85)
axL.set_aspect("equal"); axL.set_title("Centered (correlated, tilted)")
axL.set_xlabel("duration $-\\bar{x}$ (min)"); axL.set_ylabel("wait $-\\bar{x}$ (min)")
axR.scatter(W[:, 0], W[:, 1], color=ACCENT, s=28, alpha=0.85)
axR.set_aspect("equal"); axR.set_title("Whitened (round, uncorrelated)")
axR.set_xlabel("$w_1$"); axR.set_ylabel("$w_2$")
th = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 100)
axR.plot(np.cos(th), np.sin(th), color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.2)  # unit circle
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"covariance of whitened data (should be ~ identity) =\n{cov_white}")
```

The printed covariance of the whitened data is the $2\times2$ identity to numerical
precision: the ellipse has become a circle. This single transform underlies three distinct
downstream methods — GLS in regression ([REGR-11]), the change of variables that standardizes
a Gaussian in probability ([PROB-17], [PROB-18]), and the preconditioning that fixes
ill-conditioned optimization (Optimization track) — all of them the inverse matrix square
root of [GEOM-19] wearing different costumes.

# Worked example II: when the geometry strains — conditioning and a near-degenerate fit {#sec-conditioning-example}

The honest edge of this whole toolbox is **conditioning**. Every method above is a *solve*
or a *decomposition*, and every solve inherits the condition number of its matrix. We now
build a regression that is mathematically perfectly well-posed yet numerically treacherous,
watch the geometry predict the trouble, and apply the fix — chaining the condition number
([GEOM-26]), the spectral theorem ([GEOM-16]), and ridge's eigenvalue-lifting.

We fit $y$ on two predictors that are *almost* the same column — near-collinear, the cloud's
column space nearly a line instead of a plane. This is the multicollinearity of @sec-map-cond,
and it is the single most common way a real regression quietly produces garbage.

```{python}
#| label: fig-conditioning
#| fig-cap: "The edge of applicability: near-collinear predictors. **Left:** the two predictor columns $x_1, x_2$ are almost parallel (correlation near 1), so the design's column space is a nearly-degenerate sliver. **Right:** as collinearity tightens (correlation $\\to 1$), the condition number $\\kappa(X^\\top X)$ explodes and the fitted coefficients swing wildly across bootstrap resamples (shaded band = coefficient range), even though the *prediction* stays fine. Ridge ($+\\lambda I$) lifts the smallest eigenvalue off the floor and tames both. This is multicollinearity seen as pure geometry."
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 120
x1 = rng.normal(0, 1, n)

def make_design(rho):
    # x2 = rho-correlated copy of x1
    x2 = rho * x1 + np.sqrt(max(1 - rho**2, 1e-12)) * rng.normal(0, 1, n)
    X = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), x1, x2])
    return X, x2

# true relationship uses both, but they are nearly redundant
beta_true = np.array([0.5, 1.0, 1.0])

rhos = np.array([0.5, 0.9, 0.99, 0.999, 0.9999])
kappas, coef_spread = [], []
for rho in rhos:
    X, x2 = make_design(rho)
    XtX = X.T @ X
    kappas.append(np.linalg.cond(XtX))
    # bootstrap the slope-on-x1 to see its instability
    slopes = []
    for _ in range(200):
        idx = rng.integers(0, n, n)
        Xb = X[idx]; yb = Xb @ beta_true + rng.normal(0, 0.3, n)
        bhat = np.linalg.lstsq(Xb, yb, rcond=None)[0]
        slopes.append(bhat[1])
    coef_spread.append(np.std(slopes))
kappas = np.array(kappas); coef_spread = np.array(coef_spread)

# also show the ridge fix at the worst rho
rho_bad = 0.9999
Xb, _ = make_design(rho_bad)
XtX = Xb.T @ Xb
lams = np.array([0, 1e-3, 1e-2, 1e-1, 1.0])
kappa_ridge = [np.linalg.cond(XtX + l*np.eye(3)) for l in lams]

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 4.0))
Xv, x2v = make_design(0.99)
axL.scatter(Xv[:, 1], x2v, color=VEC, s=18, alpha=0.7)
axL.set_title("Two near-parallel predictors ($\\rho=0.99$)")
axL.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axL.set_ylabel("$x_2$"); axL.set_aspect("equal")

axR.plot(kappas, coef_spread, "o-", color=HILITE, label="coef. instability (boot. SD)")
axR.set_xscale("log"); axR.set_yscale("log")
axR.set_xlabel(r"$\kappa(X^\top X)$  (log)"); axR.set_ylabel("SD of fitted slope (log)")
axR.set_title("Big $\\kappa$ $\\Rightarrow$ unstable coefficients")
axR.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print("rho      kappa(X'X)        bootstrap SD of slope")
for r, k, s in zip(rhos, kappas, coef_spread):
    print(f"{r:<8.4f} {k:>14.1f}    {s:>10.4f}")
print()
print("ridge fix at rho = 0.9999:")
print("lambda     kappa(X'X + lambda I)")
for l, k in zip(lams, kappa_ridge):
    print(f"{l:<8.3g}  {k:>14.1f}")
```

The printed table is the entire multicollinearity story in numbers. As the predictor
correlation climbs from $0.5$ toward $0.9999$, the condition number of $X^\top X$ grows by
orders of magnitude, and in lockstep the bootstrap standard deviation of the fitted slope —
how much the coefficient jumps when the data are resampled — blows up. The coefficients are
not *wrong*; they are *unstable*, which is worse, because a single fit gives no hint of it.
The geometry diagnosed it in advance: near-parallel columns mean a tiny smallest eigenvalue
of $X^\top X$, hence a huge $\kappa$, hence — by the error-amplification bound of [GEOM-26] —
a solution that small data perturbations swing wildly.

The ridge table shows the fix as pure spectral arithmetic. Ridge replaces $X^\top X$ with
$X^\top X + \lambda I$. Adding $\lambda I$ adds $\lambda$ to *every* eigenvalue, and here is
the one line that shows it: $X^\top X$ is symmetric, so by the spectral theorem ([GEOM-16])
it factors as $X^\top X = Q\Lambda Q^\top$ with $Q$ orthonormal ($Q^\top Q = I$). Since
$I = QQ^\top$, we can write $\lambda I = Q(\lambda I)Q^\top$, and therefore
$$
X^\top X + \lambda I = Q\Lambda Q^\top + Q(\lambda I)Q^\top
= Q\,(\Lambda + \lambda I)\,Q^\top ,
$$
which is the spectral decomposition of the ridge-modified matrix with the *same*
eigenvectors $Q$ and each eigenvalue shifted from $\lambda_k$ to $\lambda_k + \lambda$. So
the smallest eigenvalue rises from near-zero $\lambda_{\min}$ to $\lambda_{\min} + \lambda
\geq \lambda$, while the largest barely moves, and the condition number
$(\lambda_{\max}+\lambda)/(\lambda_{\min}+\lambda)$ collapses toward a sane value — exactly
what the printed $\kappa$ column shows as $\lambda$ grows. Regression owns the statistical price of this trade
(it introduces bias — [REGR-14]); Geometry explains *why it works at all*: it is eigenvalue
lifting, visible only because we can see $X^\top X$ through its spectral decomposition.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Do not test for trouble by checking whether $\det(X^\top X) \approx 0$, and do not solve
by literally forming $(X^\top X)^{-1}$.** Both are the classic numerical traps [GEOM-26]
warned about. The determinant conflates *scale* with *conditioning* — a well-conditioned
matrix scaled down has a tiny determinant and a benign $\kappa$, while a treacherous matrix
can have a determinant near 1. The condition number, not the determinant, is the singularity
diagnostic. And forming the normal equations $X^\top X$ *squares* the condition number of
$X$ (because $\kappa(X^\top X) = \kappa(X)^2$ from the SVD, [GEOM-26]), so whenever
collinearity is even mild you should solve the least-squares problem through the **QR**
([GEOM-07]) or **SVD** ([GEOM-20]) of $X$ directly, never by inverting $X^\top X$. This is the
"solve, don't invert" discipline made specific to regression.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"A colleague's regression has wildly different coefficients every time they refit on a new
batch, but the predictions are fine. What's going on and what do you do?"* This is the exact
scenario of @fig-conditioning, and the answer is a geometry answer. The predictors are
near-collinear, so the column space is a near-degenerate sliver: the *projection* (the fitted
values, the predictions) is stable because it only depends on the subspace, but the
*coordinates of that projection in the predictor basis* (the coefficients) are unstable
because the basis is nearly degenerate. Diagnose it with the condition number of $X^\top X$
or a variance-inflation factor, not the determinant. Fix it by removing or combining the
redundant predictors, by regularizing (ridge lifts the smallest eigenvalue), or by switching
to a PCA/SVD basis that is orthogonal by construction. The deeper point you should volunteer:
*prediction and inference have different conditioning* — the same model can be trustworthy
for one and useless for the other.
:::

# When the geometry is the wrong lens {#sec-failure}

An honest bridge must say where the bridge ends. The linear-algebraic geometry this track
built is the right lens for an enormous swath of data science, but it has a definite
boundary, and a graduate-level practitioner names it without being asked.

- **The structure is genuinely nonlinear.** Every tool here finds *linear* structure —
  subspaces, principal *axes*, linear maps. If the data lie on a curved manifold (a spiral, a
  swiss roll), the best line or plane is a poor summary, and PCA's top components miss the
  real geometry entirely. The fix lives one level up: kernel methods ([UNSP-09], and the
  kernel trick owned by Classification), manifold learning ([UNSP-10], [UNSP-11]), and
  nonparametric regression. The geometric *idea* survives — we project in a richer feature
  space — but the plain linear tools do not.
- **The relationship is not about second-order structure at all.** Covariance, the spectral
  theorem, and PCA only ever see *means, variances, and linear correlations* — the first two
  moments. Two variables can be strongly dependent with zero correlation (e.g.
  $Y = X^2$ with symmetric $X$), and a covariance matrix is blind to it. When dependence is
  higher-order or distributional, you need mutual information, copulas, or distributional
  tests, not eigenvectors.
- **The matrix is not symmetric, or the problem is not a quadratic.** The cleanest results in
  the track — the spectral theorem, the bowl-shaped quadratic, the unique minimum — are
  promises about *symmetric* matrices and *quadratic* objectives. A general loss surface has
  no orthonormal eigenbasis and many critical points; a non-symmetric map can have complex
  eigenvalues and no real invariant axes ([GEOM-14] flagged this). The SVD is the tool that
  survives generality ([GEOM-20]), which is exactly why it is the master decomposition — but
  even it only linearizes.
- **The data are not numeric, or distances are meaningless.** The entire edifice rests on
  a cloud of points in $\mathbb{R}^n$ with a meaningful dot product ([GEOM-00], [GEOM-04]). For
  categorical data, graphs, text, or sets, the right geometry is a *chosen* embedding or
  kernel, and the linear tools apply only after that choice — which is itself the hard part,
  and not a linear-algebra question.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"PCA gave me components that explain 95% of the variance but my downstream classifier got
worse. Why?"* Because **variance is not relevance.** PCA keeps the directions of largest
*spread*, but the discriminative signal — the direction that separates classes — may lie in a
low-variance direction that PCA discards. PCA is unsupervised; it cannot see the label. This
is the honest limit of "axes of the cloud": the cloud's longest axis need not be the axis
that matters for your task. When the goal is separation, reach for a *supervised* projection
(linear discriminant analysis, owned by Classification) instead. The interviewer is testing
whether you know that "captures the variance" and "captures the signal" are different claims.
:::

# Interview-lens roundup {#sec-interview}

The standard linear-algebra interview surface for a data-science role is small and
predictable, and every question on it is one we have now earned an answer to. A compact
reference, each answered in one move with its applicability judgment.

- **"What is least squares, geometrically?"** Projection of $y$ onto the column space of $X$;
  the residual is orthogonal to every predictor (the normal equations). Use it when the
  relationship is linear, errors are roughly homoscedastic, and no point dominates; reach for
  robust loss, GLS, or nonparametric methods otherwise ([GEOM-06], [REGR-04]).
- **"What is PCA actually computing?"** The eigenvectors of the covariance matrix (equivalently
  the right singular vectors of the centered data), kept in order of eigenvalue size. It finds
  the directions of maximum variance. Use it for compression, denoising, and visualization;
  do **not** use it as feature selection for a supervised task, because variance is not
  relevance ([GEOM-16], [GEOM-20], [UNSP-07]).
- **"Why is the covariance matrix always positive-semidefinite, and why does it matter?"**
  Because $u^\top \Sigma u$ is the variance of the projection onto $u$, which cannot be
  negative; it is positive-*definite* iff no linear combination of the variables is constant.
  It matters because PD is exactly the condition for a valid Gaussian, an invertible
  whitening transform, and a Cholesky factor ([GEOM-18], [GEOM-19]).
- **"What does the SVD give you that the eigendecomposition does not?"** It exists for *every*
  matrix — rectangular, rank-deficient, anything — with real nonnegative singular values and
  orthonormal $U, V$. The eigendecomposition needs a square matrix with a full eigenbasis
  (and is only orthonormal when the matrix is symmetric). Reach for the SVD for rank, the
  pseudoinverse, low-rank approximation, and numerically stable least squares ([GEOM-20],
  [GEOM-21]).
- **"How do you know an optimization will converge quickly / a regression is trustworthy?"**
  Look at the conditioning: the eigenvalue ratio of the Hessian for optimization, the
  condition number of $X^\top X$ for regression. A long thin valley (large $\kappa$) means
  slow gradient descent and unstable coefficients; preconditioning, ridge, or a better basis
  fix it ([GEOM-24], [GEOM-26]).
- **"What is Fisher information, in one sentence?"** The (expected) curvature of the
  log-likelihood at its peak — a Hessian — so high curvature means the data pin the parameter
  down tightly. It is the bridge from the second-derivative geometry of this track to the
  variance of an estimator ([GEOM-24], [INFR-B3]).
- **"When should you NOT use these linear tools?"** When the structure is nonlinear, when
  dependence is higher-order than correlation, when distances on the raw data are meaningless,
  or when you need separation rather than spread. Name the alternative each time — kernels,
  manifold learning, mutual information, LDA (@sec-failure).

::: {.callout-important title="The one-line translations to memorize"}
- **Fit a model** = project onto the column space ([GEOM-06]).
- **Find structure** = spectral-decompose the covariance / SVD the data ([GEOM-16], [GEOM-20]).
- **Decorrelate or simulate** = inverse / forward matrix square root ([GEOM-19]).
- **Train** = descend the gradient, read curvature from the Hessian ([GEOM-23], [GEOM-24]).
- **Trust the answer?** = read the condition number ([GEOM-26]).
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track — and where you go now"}
This is the **terminal article** of the Geometry-of-Data track. You arrived having built and
proved every tool in isolation; you leave able to recognize each one inside the methods of
every other track. There is no [GEOM-28] — the track's job is done. The hand-offs:

- **Regression.** [REGR-04] is "least squares = projection" ([GEOM-06]); [REGR-05] is the
  matrix normal equations @eq-normal-multi; [REGR-07] is the condition number of $X^\top X$
  ([GEOM-26]); [REGR-11] is GLS via the matrix square root ([GEOM-19]); [REGR-14] is ridge as
  eigenvalue lifting ([GEOM-16]). Every one links back here for the algebra.
- **Statistical Inference.** [INFR-B3] builds Fisher information on the Hessian-as-curvature
  picture ([GEOM-24], [GEOM-25]); the multivariate normal and its quadratic-form exponent ride
  on positive-definiteness ([GEOM-18]).
- **Optimization.** Consumes the gradient ([GEOM-23]), the Hessian and Newton step
  ([GEOM-24], [GEOM-25]), and the condition number as the convergence-rate driver
  ([GEOM-26]); it owns the *algorithms*, this track owns the *geometry* they walk on.
- **Unsupervised Learning.** [UNSP-07]/[UNSP-08] build PCA on the spectral theorem and the SVD
  ([GEOM-16], [GEOM-20], [GEOM-21]); the kernel and manifold articles ([UNSP-09]–[UNSP-11])
  are where the geometry goes *nonlinear*, exactly the boundary of @sec-failure.
- **Probability foundations.** [PROB-17]/[PROB-18] use the covariance matrix, linear
  transforms of random vectors, and whitening — the statistics on top of the symmetric-PSD
  and matrix-square-root machinery here.
- **Classification** and **Causal Inference** consume projection, the SVD, and quadratic forms
  throughout (LDA, kernel methods, regression adjustment) and link back rather than
  re-deriving.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The book that made the geometric view canonical.** Gilbert Strang, *Introduction to
  Linear Algebra* (and his MIT 18.06 lectures) — the four-subspaces, "matrix = action on a
  cloud" pedagogy that this entire track is downstream of. Strang's *Linear Algebra and
  Learning from Data* (Wellesley-Cambridge, 2019) is the closest single source to *this very
  article*: a working data scientist's map from the algebra to the methods.
- **The decomposition-first reference.** Lloyd N. Trefethen and David Bau, *Numerical Linear
  Algebra* (SIAM 1997) — least squares as projection, the SVD as the organizing object, and
  conditioning treated as a first-class citizen, all in the geometric spirit used here.
- **The historical roots of the costumes.** Karl Pearson, "On lines and planes of closest fit
  to systems of points in space," *Philosophical Magazine* (1901), and Harold Hotelling,
  "Analysis of a complex of statistical variables into principal components," *J. Educational
  Psychology* (1933) — PCA invented as a geometry of clouds, decades before "eigenvectors of a
  covariance matrix" was the phrase. R. A. Fisher's information matrix and Gauss's *Theoria
  Motus* (least squares) complete the picture of applications preceding the unifying algebra.
- **Open threads this track deliberately left to its consumers.** *How do we choose the number
  of PCA components in practice?* — scree plots and cross-validation, [UNSP-08]. *What exactly
  is the convergence rate of gradient descent in terms of $\kappa$?* — Optimization, where the
  rate $(\kappa-1)/(\kappa+1)$ (and the $\sqrt{\kappa}$ improvement of conjugate gradients) is
  derived. *How do we make these tools nonlinear without abandoning the geometry?* — the
  kernel trick (Classification) and manifold learning ([UNSP-10]–[UNSP-11]), where projection
  and the SVD reappear in a feature space we never coordinate explicitly. The bridge ends
  here; each of those is the first plank of the next.
